coredata.dc:title: Engineering particles for sensing applications via in-situ synthesizing carbon dots@SiO2 photonic crystals

coredata.dc:description: Particle engineering has been one of the hotspots in both stimuli-responsive and smart nanomaterials. However, efficient and facile implementation of robust covalent interaction in particle engineering remains a great challenge. Herein, we firstly propose an easy-to-perform and versatile bottom-up particle engineering strategy for covalent coupling between SiO2 particle and carbon dots (CDs) in situ. Robust covalent interactions enable the nano-micro coupling of CDs and SiO2 particles, and nearly twofold fluorescence enhancement of CDs was obtained. Moreover, hydrogen bond-driven assembly of the carbon dots@SiO2 (CDs@SiO2) particles was demonstrated via molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, which guaranteed the high-quality colloidal photonic crystals (CPCs) without coffee ring effect. In addition, the optical coupling between CPCs and CDs ensures brighter and more saturated structural colors, as well as enhanced fluorescence for microfluidics-derived bi-optical CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs. Based on this, bi-optical signal detection chip towards veterinary drug is developed. The in-situ covalent coupling strategy developed in this work might open an innovative avenue towards the particle engineering, which will also provide a methodological reference for the design of functional inorganic particles.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the CDs@SiO2 particles via the one-pot hydrothermal strategy. Strong covalent interactions enable the robust decoration of CDs onto SiO2 particles. (b) Illustration of the fluorescence enhancement mechanism in CDs@SiO2 with improved light collection capacity. (c) Schematic diagrams of the assembly of pure SiO2 particle and the CDs@SiO2 particles. SiO2 particles prefer forming coffee-ring, whereas, anchor-enhanced assembly of CDs@SiO2 particles tends to construct plate-like CPCs and appear bright structural color. (d) Bi-optical signal CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs were constructed using the droplet microfluidic technology. (e) Bi-optical signal CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs could be employed as optical sensor array for the development of microfluidic chip detector towards veterinary drug concentration online detection.

(a) FT-IR spectra of the pure SiO2 particles (black), pure CDs (blue) and CDs@SiO2 particles (red). (b) HRTEM image of the CDs@SiO2 particle. Inset: crystal lattices of the CDs. (c) UV–vis absorption (red) and PL spectra (black) of the CDs@SiO2 (precursor concentration: 0.05 wt%) latex. (d) PL spectra (excitation wavelength: 370 nm, excitation voltage: 550 V) of pure CDs and CDs@SiO2 latexes (precursor: CA and urea) under same preparation conditions. Insert: fluorescent images of pure CDs (left) and CDs@SiO2 latex (right). (e) PL spectra (excitation wavelength: 370 nm, excitation voltage: 550 V) and optical images of mixture of CDs and SiO2 latex (1) before and (2) after centrifugation, of CDs@SiO2 latex via one-pot hydrothermal strategy (3) before and (4) after centrifugation. (f) PL spectra (excitation wavelength: 370 nm, excitation voltage: 550 V) of fresh CDs@SiO2 latex and latex stored for 7, 21, and 35 days. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) PL spectra of pure CDs and CDs@SiO2 latexes (excitation wavelength: 420 nm, excitation voltage: 550 V) under same preparation conditions (precursor: CA and urea, precursor concentration: 7.5 wt%). Insert: fluorescent images of pure CDs (up) and CDs@SiO2 latex (down). (b) PL spectra of pure CDs and CDs@SiO2 latexes (excitation wavelength: 370 nm, excitation voltage: 500 V) under same preparation conditions (precursor: CA and ethanediamine, precursor concentration: 0.05 wt%). Insert: fluorescent images of pure CDs (up) and CDs@SiO2 latex (down). (c) PL spectra (excitation wavelength: 370 nm, excitation voltage: 500 V) of CDs@SiO2 latexes by varying the SiO2 particle sizes from 200 to 600 nm under same preparation conditions (precursor: CA and urea, precursor concentration: 0.05 wt%). (d) Decay time and QY of the CDs@SiO2 latexes with varied SiO2 particle sizes. (e) Schematic shows the electromagnetic scattering filed of the SiO2 dielectric particles, which enables the enhancement of fluorescence emission of CDs. (f) Size-dependent fluorescence enhancement effect of SiO2 dielectric particles.

(a) Micro-IR images confirm the anchor-enhanced assembly of CDs@SiO2 CPCs based on these characteristic absorption peaks. (b) Optical microscope and SEM images of pure SiO2 and CDs@SiO2 CPCs. (c) Reflection spectra of pure SiO2 and the CDs@SiO2 CPCs. Insets: the corresponding optical photographs. (d) Normalized reflection spectra and corresponding optical images of CDs@SiO2 CPCs with vivid structural colors covering the entire visible spectrum. (e) Time-series photographs of the assembly process of pure SiO2 and CDs@SiO2 droplets on solid substrate. Scale bar is 500 µm. (f) Static WCA of the pure SiO2 and CDs@SiO2 CPC films. (g) Equilibrium configuration of a nanoparticle-laden nanodroplet on the solid substrate. Deposit morphologies of nanoparticle-laden nanodroplet with varied particle–fluid εPW and particle–particle εPP interaction parameters on the solid substrate. (h) Corresponding characterizations of particle distribution.

(a) Optical and fluorescence micrographs of pure SiO2 CPC supraballs (204 nm) and CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs (204, 246, and 277 nm). (b) PL spectra of the pure CDs and reflection spectra of CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs with different SiO2 particle sizes (204, 246 and 277 nm). (c) PL spectra of CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs with different SiO2 particle size (204, 246 and 277 nm). (d) Optical and fluorescence micrographs of blue CDs@SiO2 CPC supraball corresponding to different MG concentration (CMG from 0 to 15 × 10−6 mol/L). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Stem-Volmer curve of CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs towards MG concentration. (b) Time-resolved PL decay curves of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs (reflection peak: 452 nm) corresponding to different MG concentration (CMG from 0 to 15 × 10−6 mol/L). (c) Design of multichannel microfluidic online detection chip based on the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraball. (d) Detection mechanism of the bi-optical signal CDs@SiO2 CPC supraball. Redshift phenomenon in reflection and the quenching in fluorescence are contributed to the incorporation of MG. (e) Optical and fluorescence photographs of the microfluidic chip detector in the response state to MG concentration (CMG from 0 to 15 × 10−6 mol/L). Scale bar is 5 mm. Response of (f) reflectance wavelength and (g) PL intensity of the microfluidic chip detector under different MG concentration (CMG from 0 to 15 × 10−6 mol/L).

head.ce:title: Engineering particles for sensing applications via in-situ synthesizing carbon dots@SiO2 photonic crystals

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, 5 Xin Mofan Road, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Particle engineering has been one of the hotspots in both stimuli-responsive and smart nanomaterials. However, efficient and facile implementation of robust covalent interaction in particle engineering remains a great challenge. Herein, we firstly propose an easy-to-perform and versatile bottom-up particle engineering strategy for covalent coupling between SiO2 particle and carbon dots (CDs) in situ. Robust covalent interactions enable the nano-micro coupling of CDs and SiO2 particles, and nearly twofold fluorescence enhancement of CDs was obtained. Moreover, hydrogen bond-driven assembly of the carbon dots@SiO2 (CDs@SiO2) particles was demonstrated via molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, which guaranteed the high-quality colloidal photonic crystals (CPCs) without coffee ring effect. In addition, the optical coupling between CPCs and CDs ensures brighter and more saturated structural colors, as well as enhanced fluorescence for microfluidics-derived bi-optical CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs. Based on this, bi-optical signal detection chip towards veterinary drug is developed. The in-situ covalent coupling strategy developed in this work might open an innovative avenue towards the particle engineering, which will also provide a methodological reference for the design of functional inorganic particles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Engineering nanoparticles for sensing and biomedical applications are one of hotspots for both fundamental and applied chemistry [1–3]. Great efforts have been made in pursuing rational synthesis, surface modification and functionalization of nano-scale particles, including ligand-modified method [4], coupling technique [5], and doping modification [6]. For instance, Nie et al. investigated the influences of typical organic ligands on the peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation performance of inorganic metal-based LaMnO3 particles [7]. Zhou et al. presented a facile supramolecular assembly of natural polyphenols and aromatic dithiol to chemically engineer diverse particles [8]. Recently, Kim and co-workers [9] introduced a solvent-free large-scale surface modification method for inorganic particles based on the mechanical grinding-driven polysiloxanes (PSO) brush grafting reaction. Obviously, robust covalent interaction, which could ensure stable chemical coupling between particles and modifier, are a high concern for efficient nanoparticle engineering. The strong covalent interaction also is crucial for further application of functional particles, which can successfully avoid the performance attenuation caused by the stripping of functional groups. On the contrary, it is difficult to achieve this goal by employing weak interaction such as physical adsorption, hydrogen bonding, and stacking interaction [10–12]. However, it seems not to be easy to carry out the covalent interaction on inorganic nanoparticles since their surface is seldom to functional groups. Thus, the high-efficient and facile strategy towards robust covalent interaction in inorganic particle engineering is highly desirable.

Monodispersed SiO2 particles have been regarded as an essential building block for colloidal photonic crystal (CPCs), which show superior properties of temperature and chemical resistance as well as high-order Fröhlich resonances [13–15]. Especially, particle engineering based on covalent coupling for monodispersed SiO2 particles have well been developed for further modifying the structural and functional performances of CPCs. In this regard, Ge et al. proposed superhydrophobic CPC film based on the reaction of the hydrophilic SiO2 particles with (heptadecafluoro-1,1,2,2,-tetrahydrodecyl) dimethylchlorosilane (HDFTHD) [16]. Chen et al. grafted fluorescent Cu-In-S/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) on SiO2 particles by using dendrimers as bridges to construct CPCs, which could display stable structural color and fluorescence [17]. Additionally, our group [18] introduced light-absorbing groups (methyl viologen moieties) onto SiO2 particles by silane chemistry for functionalized SiO2 CPCs with enhanced color saturation and tunable structural colors. Despite indisputable achievements in this field, most of these strategies, however, require the aid of a couple-crosslinking agent or a group activator. It’s seem to be far from easy to implement due to tedious procedures. Therefore, a facile and feasible in-situ particle engineering strategy is vital for promoting the development of SiO2 CPCs.

CPCs with unique optical properties, which are derived from the photonic band gap (PBG), have been widely applied in optical displays and colorimetric sensors. In this regard, fluorescent CPCs, have gained wide concern in recent years based on the synergetic fluorescent and structural colors [19,20]. Researchers have devoted great efforts to develop fluorescent CPCs, for example, infiltrating the inverse-opal structure with fluorescent material [21–23], or recombination of colloid particles and as-prepared fluorescent material [24,25]. However, the major obstacles still are tedious preparation procedures and insufficient interaction force, severely limiting their practical applications. In this work, we firstly present an easy-to-perform and versatile bottom-up particle engineering strategy for ensuring covalent coupling between SiO2 particle and carbon dots (CDs) in situ, to construct robust CDs@SiO2 fluorescent CPCs. This work offers the following attractive advantages: (1) We develop an available one-pot particle engineering method by inducing the in-situ reaction of SiO2 monodispersed particles and precursor of CDs (citric acid (CA) and urea), where strong covalent coupling occurs between CDs and SiO2 during the synthesis process of CDs. Typically, the coupling is derived from the reaction between hydroxyl group of SiO2 particles and the carboxyl group of CDs, enabling the robust nano-micro interconnection (Fig. 1
a). (2) Based on the robust covalent interactions and electromagnetic resonance premise of the SiO2 particles, fluorescence intensity of CDs was highly enhanced (Fig. 1 b). (3) It is very promising that the hydrogen bond-driven assembly of the CDs@SiO2 particles were demonstrated to ensure high-quality CPCs without coffee ring effect (Fig. 1 c). (4) Optical coupling between CPCs and CDs achieves brighter and more saturated structural colors, as well as enhanced fluorescence for microfluidics-derived CDs@SiO2 bi-optical CPC supraballs. And the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs exhibit bi-optical responsiveness of fluorescence and structural color (Fig. 1 d). Thus, bi-optical signal detection chip is also constructed based on the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs, which shows distinct advantages of high-precision in online detection towards veterinary drug (Fig. 1 e). We therefore believe that the in-situ covalent coupling method developed in this work provides a facile and efficient pathway for the particle engineering, which will also offer a methodological guidance for the development of high-performance and functional CPCs.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), ammonium hydroxide, ethanol, CA, urea, ethanediamine, methylsilicone oil, malachite green (MG), pefloxacin (PEF), methylene blue (MB), and crystal violet (CV) were obtained from Aladdin Reagents Co., Ltd and applied directly without further purification. The resistivity of deionized water used in laboratory is 18 MΩ/cm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of monodispersed SiO2 colloidal latex
ce:section.ce:para: Monodispersed SiO2 colloidal latex with hydroxyl group was synthesized via the modified Stöber method. TEOS (2 g) was hydrolyzed into small silica particles (30–40 nm) under the catalysis of ammonium hydroxide (1.6 g) as seed solution. TEOS and ammonium hydroxide were added in drops simultaneously into the mixture of ethanol (200 mL) and seed solution (3 g). SiO2 particles were gradually grown to the desired size through the hydrolysis and condensation of TEOS. The obtained SiO2 colloidal latex was washed twice by centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 8 min, and then dispersed in deionized water for subsequent experiments.

A facile one-pot hydrothermal process was conducted to fabricate the CDs@SiO2 latex. Typically, homogeneous aqueous solution (20 mL) of CA (0.005 g), urea (0.005 g) and SiO2 colloidal latex (5 g, 20 wt%) were transferred into a 50 mL autoclave. The whole system was left for reaction at 180 °C for 4 h, which was then cooled to room temperature, resulting in a yellowish CDs@SiO2 latex. The latex was centrifuged 2 times at 7000 rpm for 8 min and collected after filtrating with a membrane (0.24 μm). The urea (0.005 g) precursor in the homogeneous aqueous solution was replaced with ethanediamine (0.005 g). While the other reaction parameters kept intact. We can prepare the CDs@SiO2 latex with high quantum yield (QY, ∼70%) [26]. Meanwhile, The homogeneous aqueous solution (4 mL) of CA (0.15 g), urea (0.15 g) and SiO2 colloidal latex (1 g, 20 wt%) were transferred into a 200 °C drying oven for 4 h. We can obtain a green fluorescent CDs@SiO2 latex [27].

The corresponding pure CDs without SiO2 particles were prepared as control sample via the same hydrothermal process. For example, the mixture of CA (0.005 g), urea (0.005 g) and deionized water (20 mL) were added into 50 mL autoclave. The autoclave was placed in a drying oven under 180 °C for 4 h. Then the product was filtered by filter (0.24 μm) for subsequent use.

CPC films could be assembled on substrates. Specifically, plasma surface modified glass sheets (1000 W, 180 s) were used as the substrate. Concentrated CDs@SiO2 latex (25–40 wt%) was quickly cast onto the substrate, and spread with the 9 μm-depth wire-wound rod coater. After water evaporation, 2-D CPC film displaying bright structural color were attained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs
ce:section.ce:para: A capillary microfluidic chip was obtained from Nanjing Janus New Materials Co., Ltd for the construction of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs. CDs@SiO2 latex (solid content 25 wt%) as inner fluid (flow velocities 0.1 mL·h−1) and methylsilicone oil as outer fluid (flow velocities 25 mL·h−1) were pumped into the microfluidic chip by sophisticated syringe pumps (Nanjing Janus New Materials Co., Ltd). CDs@SiO2 latex continuously forms uniform microdroplets in the chip under the action of shear force. After evaporation self-assembly, the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs with dual optical properties of stable fluorescence and bright structural color were realized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Manufacture of bi-optical signal detection chip
ce:section.ce:para: PMMA panel, 2 mm thick, with lily petal shaped chamber (depth 500 μm) and interconnected channels (diameter 200 μm and depth 200 μm) was carved by engraving machine (Beijing Jingdiao Technology Group Co. Ltd) as template. CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs with different PBGs were orderly implanted in chamber of the template to obtain bi-optical signal detection chip with structural color and fluorescence responses. 500 μL MG solution of a certain concentration was injected into the detection chip using pipette. The concentration of MG can be determined by comparing and analyzing the changed structural color and fluorescence of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs in the chip.

Micro-IR images and spectra were collected by Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iN10). The element component of particles was analyzed using energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The crystal structural analysis of particles was recorded via X-Ray diffraction (XRD) (Bruker-AXS D8 ADVANCE). The microstructures of CDs@SiO2 particle were shot by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL JEM-2100). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra, lifetime and QY were characterized by fluorescence spectrometer (FLS 980-STM). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern, ZEN3690) was used to confirm the hydrodynamic diameter distribution and polydispersity index (PDI) of the resultant latex. The morphologies and assembly of particles were observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM) (HITACHI S-4800). UV–vis absorption spectra were collected via UV–vis spectrometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 900). Reflection spectra of assembled CPCs were measured by an optical fiber spectrometer (USB4000). Fluorescence images and structural color of samples were shot using stereo microscope (SMZ800). The hydrophilicity of CPC film was assessed with a static water contact angle (WCA) analyzer (KRÜSS, DSA100).

MD simulations were further performed to study the evaporation of nanoparticle-laden nanodroplet on a heated solid surface. Following the models developed by Zhang et al. [28], the liquid, solid and nanoparticle were built by three species of Lennard-Jones atoms respectively, i.e. W, S and P, which interact with each other by the non-bonded 12–6 potential in a cut-off distance

More details for the models and simulations can be found in Supporting Information.

We aim to develop an easy-to-perform and feasible particle engineering strategy enabling the covalent coupling between SiO2 and fluorescent particles for functional CPCs. To this end, we mixed monodispersed SiO2 particles and precursor of CDs (CA and urea) in an autoclave, which undergone a hydrothermal treatment under 180 °C for 4 h to obtain robust CDs@SiO2 particles. In theory, the high thermal stability of SiO2 particles (melting point of 1610 °C) can ensure the morphology [30], which remains basically unchanged after hydrothermal treatment (180 °C, 4 h) ( Fig. S1 ). In addition, under the high temperature and pressure atmosphere of the hydrothermal reaction, CDs would be generated due to the reaction of CA and urea [31]. Meanwhile, the covalent coupling of CDs and SiO2 particles occurs [32], thereby allowing the one-pot preparation of CDs@SiO2 particles. As a proof of concept, we conducted the hydrothermal experiments in an autoclave, where a mixture of SiO2 latex and precursor (CA and urea) with different concentrations were used. As a result, with the precursor concentration increases, the color of the latex is deepened gradually, from the original milky white (0 wt%) to brown (0.15 wt%) (Fig. S2
a). Hydrodynamic diameter distribution of the synthesized CDs@SiO2 latex was analyzed (Fig. S2 b and c). The size of CDs@SiO2 particles keeps almost constant when the precursor concentration is less than 0.05 wt%, whereas these latexes can still maintain low PDI (<0.05). The preferred size distribution ensures that these latexes can be used as building blocks for high-quality CPCs (Fig. S2 d) [33]. While excessive precursor (concentration above 0.05 wt%) breaks the balance of the latex and causes the agglomeration and precipitation of particles, which blocks the assembly for long-range ordered CPCs. And as expected, this CDs@SiO2 latexes appear blue fluorescence. However, the fluorescence shows signs of fading for the CDs@SiO2 latex when the precursor concentration exceeds 0.07 wt%, which can be attributed to the aggregation-caused quenching of CDs (Fig. S2 e).

To confirm the successful decoration of the CDs onto the monodispersed SiO2 particles, we conducted various characterization analyses. Fig. 2 a presents FT-IR spectra of pure SiO2 latex, pure CDs, and CDs@SiO2 latex. For CDs, 1560 cm−1 and 1722 cm−1 are corresponded to N-H stretching vibrations of amino and C = O stretching vibrations of carboxyl, respectively [34]. While for SiO2, the peak at 3429 cm−1 is the O-H stretching vibrations. And characteristic peaks at 1109 cm−1 and 794 cm−1 are observed, which belong to the Si-O-Si and Si-O stretching vibrations, respectively [35,36]. Obviously, all these characteristic peaks can be observed on the FT-IR spectrum of CDs@SiO2 latex. Besides, the appearance of C = O stretching vibrations peak at 1754 cm−1 and the weaken O-H stretching vibrations at 3429 cm−1 on the CDs@SiO2 FT-IR spectrum confirmed the covalent coupling between CDs and SiO2 particles (the formation of O-C = O groups). The micro-IR images also indicate the greatly enhanced distribution of N-H, O-C = O, and O-H groups of CDs@SiO2 latex through colors. ( Fig. S3 ). EDS were carried out to detect the component of pure SiO2 particles and CDs@SiO2 particles. As shown in Fig. S4 , elements including silicon and oxygen are facilely found in SiO2 particles. The occurrence of carbon element in the CDs@SiO2 particles indicates the presence of CDs. TEM was employed to observe the microstructure of resultant CDs@SiO2 particles. It should be noted that CDs (about 5 nm) are uniformly dotted on the surface of regular spherical SiO2 particles without aggregation. And the lattice fringes of CDs with about 0.34 nm lattice spacing (Fig. 2 b,
Fig. S5 ) [37]. The crystallinity of the pure SiO2 and CDs@SiO2 particle was characterized by XRD. In Fig. S6 , both pure SiO2 particles (2θ = 22°) and CDs@SiO2 particles show only one characteristic peak due to their amorphous structures. However, the characteristic diffraction peak of CDs@SiO2 particles shifts slightly to the right and that can be attributed to the introduction of CDs (2θ = 26°) [38,39]. All these results prove that CDs have been successfully decorated on the SiO2 particle surface by the novel one-pot hydrothermal strategy.

The one-pot hydrothermal strategy enables the successful covalent coupling between CDs and SiO2 colloidal particles, which will render the resultant CDs@SiO2 particles with dual optical properties of fluorescence and structural color. Obviously, according to the gradually growing absorption peaks ( Fig. S7 ), the increase of precursor concentration causes the content of CDs on the CDs@SiO2 to rise [40]. Based on this, we investigated the effect of precursor concentration (from 0 to 0.05 wt%) on the optical properties of the CDs@SiO2 particles. CPC films constructed from CDs@SiO2 particles with 0, 0.01, 0.03, and 0.05 wt% precursor concentration were displayed in Fig. S8
a-c, respectively. In terms of the pure SiO2 CPC films, the structural color is dim and whitish due to the limited refractive index (RI) (SiO2 = 1.45) and negative incoherent light scattering [41]. Clearly, with the precursor concentration increases, the structural color of CPC films becomes brighter and shinier. Compared to the pure SiO2 particles, the CDs@SiO2 particles presented distinct advantages for long-range order CPCs ( Fig. S9 ) [42]. Meanwhile, brunet CDs combines high RI with broadband light absorption that effectively compensates for the structural color deficiency of the SiO2 CPC films [43]. Whereas, the reflectance intensity of the CDs@SiO2 CPC were improved with the increase of precursor concentration, while the broad background signal presented a decreasing trend, resulting in bright and saturated color. In addition, the precursor concentration also plays an important role in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@SiO2 latex, which is also enhanced with the increasing of precursor concentration (from 0.01 to 0.05 wt%) ( Fig. S7
d and S8d). This results have been demonstrated by previous researches [44]. Comprehensively considering the structural color and fluorescence characteristics of the CDs@SiO2 particles, 0.05 wt% precursor concentration was selected in all the following experiments.

Fig. 2 c shows the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the CDs@SiO2 latex (precursor: CA and urea, precursor concentration: 0.05 wt%), where a absorption peak at around 334 nm and a strong PL emission peak centered at 445 nm were observed. Compared with pure CDs, the CDs@SiO2 latex shows brighter blue fluorescence and enhanced fluorescence intensity, which is 1.48 times higher than that of pure CDs (Fig. 2 d). Surprisingly, the QY is also improved from 30% to 37% after the covalent coupling. As seen from the time-resolved PL decay curve in Fig. S10 , both pure CDs and CDs@SiO2 latex exhibit similar mono-exponential decay [45]. And the PL lifetime of CDs@SiO2 latex is 6.65 ns, which is basically unchanged compared with that of pure CDs (6.60 ns), indicating that the coupling between SiO2 and CDs makes no difference to the photoluminescence mechanism [46]. To further verify the robust covalent coupling between CDs and SiO2 particles rather than simply mixing during the one-step hydrothermal process, we conducted comparative experiments. Specially, a homogenous mixture was used as control sample. As shown in the Fig. 2 e, the CDs@SiO2 latex displays higher PL intensity than that of the mixture of CDs and SiO2 latex. After high-speed centrifugation (16000 rpm), the supernatant of the mixture of the CDs and SiO2 latex exhibits bright blue color under UV light, and the PL intensity just shows a slight change. Interestingly, in terms of the as-prepared CDs@SiO2 latex, the precipitate exhibits bright blue color while the PL intensity of the supernatant shows a sharp decline, which demonstrates that most of the CDs precipitated along with the SiO2 particles. Thus, during the one-pot hydrothermal process, CDs can be firmly anchored on the surface of the SiO2 particles. Furthermore, we investigated the fluorescence stability of the CDs@SiO2 latex. Notably, the PL intensity and position of the CDs@SiO2 particles kept almost constant for 35 days ( Fig. 2 f), revealing the excellent fluorescence stability. Therefore, the robust covalent coupling between SiO2 particles and CDs via the novel one-pot hydrothermal strategy is favorable for the enhancement of fluorescence and structural color properties.

Another indication is that dielectric particles feature strong electromagnetic resonances, which can efficiently enhance the fluorescence intensity of coupled fluorescence emitters [47]. In our work, the covalent coupling of SiO2 and CDs (precursor: CA and urea, precursor concentration: 0.05 wt%) via the one-pot hydrothermal strategy successfully achieved nearly double fluorescence enhancement. However, the invariable emission peak position and fluorescence lifetime are completely different from the widely applicable plasma excitation theory [48]. Thus, these unexpected phenomena arouse interest in further exploration of the fluorescence enhancement mechanism. We tended to prepared a green fluorescent CDs@SiO2 latex by adjusting the precursor concentration (precursor: CA and urea, precursor concentration: 7.5 wt%), and the fluorescence intensity was also increased by more than 3 times compared with the corresponding pure CDs (Fig. 3
a). Moreover, replacing the urea precursor with ethanediamine (precursor: CA and ethanediamine, precursor concentration: 0.05 wt%), we prepared CDs@SiO2 particles with high QY (exceed 75%). And the fluorescence intensity was also enhanced, which is up to 1.6 times higher than that of pure CDs (Fig. 3 b). Meanwhile, as expected, the emission peak position and fluorescence lifetime of them did not change significantly ( Fig. S11 ). In addition, by simply changing the size of SiO2 particles from 200 to 600 nm (precursor: CA and urea, precursor concentration: 0.05 wt%), a series of CDs@SiO2 particles were constructed. Notably, the PL intensity was progressively enhanced with the size of SiO2 particles, while the PL emission peak position and fluorescence lifetime kept almost unchanged (Fig. 3 c and 3d,
Fig. S12 ). The results reveal the size dependence of dielectric particles for fluorescence enhancement [49]. Typically, the strong electromagnetic resonances of dielectric SiO2 (dielectric constant: 3.9) particle will generate strong electromagnetic field in the vicinity of particles, enhancing the fluorescence collection efficiency of coupled fluorescence emitter (Fig. 3 e). In this case, the interaction between SiO2 particles and CDs does not change the radiation properties of fluorescent emitters. Therefore, compared with pure CDs, the emission peak position and fluorescence lifetime of CDs@SiO2 particles kept unchanged. This is in complete accord with our experimental results. Obviously, as the increase of SiO2 particle size, CDs can be confined to a stronger electromagnetic field, which will contribute to a more enhanced fluorescence intensity due to the improved light collection efficiency of the CDs near the larger size SiO2 particles (Fig. 3 f). The fluorescence enhancement depends on the fluorescence collection capacity of dielectric nanoparticles, providing guidance for designing high-performance fluorescent materials.

We further explored the assembly behavior for CPCs by using the as-prepared CDs@SiO2 particles (precursor: CA and urea, precursor concentration: 0.05 wt%) as building blocks. Fig. 4
a shows the micro-IR spectra of pure SiO2 and CDs@SiO2 CPCs, respectively. Obviously, after decorating CDs, the distribution of carboxyl and amino and even ester groups (characteristic peak at 1722, 891, and 1754 cm−1, respectively) are enriched, which generate enhanced hydrogen bonding interactions, facilitating the particles to be assembled in a compact and orderly fashion (face-centered cubic (FCC) arrangement) [50]. Thus, cracks and defects in the CDs@SiO2 CPC film are effectively suppressed, and the bright and crack-free CPC film is obtained (Fig. 4 b). As seen in Fig. 4 c, the higher reflection peak and narrower full width at half maximum (FWHM) of CDs@SiO2 CPCs further confirm the highly ordered assembly of particles. The reduced background signal is mainly responsible for the improvement of structural color saturation. Monodispersed CDs@SiO2 particles with size of 204, 220, 246, 253, 277 and 291 nm were prepared via the one-pot hydrothermal strategy (precursor: CA and urea, precursor concentration: 0.05 wt%) and then were assembled into CPC films, which generate a full spectrum of structural colors depending on the particle sizes (Fig. 4 d). These structural colors can still maintain their almost original brightness for up to 35 days and retain their nearly undiminished reflective peak intensity ( Fig. S13
a). The homogeneity of CPC films was also investigated by measuring the reflection peaks at different points. As indicated in Fig. S13
b, the CPC film displays a relatively uniform distribution of reflection wavelength and intensity, illustrating the formation of homogeneous CPC film. Consequently, the robust CDs@SiO2 particles synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal strategy share distinct advantages of high-quality CPCs, which will satisfy practical applications in painting and displaying. More attractively, the CDs/SiO2 particles can be applicable to microfluidic printing for subtle CPC patterns. A microfluidic printing device with programmatically removable printing head was utilized to print a CPC pattern in a dot-to-dot fashion. As shown in Fig. S14 , fine “maple leaf” patterns with green and blue bright structural colors were successfully constructed. These printing dots in pattern exhibit virtually uniform size, plate-like morphologies and brilliant structural colors based on the ordered arrangement of the CDs/SiO2 particles.

We then thoroughly investigated the evaporation-induced self-assembly of both pure SiO2 and CDs@SiO2 particles by drying colloidal droplets on a plasma-surface treated glass substrate. As shown in Fig. 4 e, the drying of SiO2-laden droplet left a ring-like deposit, which is well known as the coffee-ring effect. In contrast, it is very promising that the CDs@SiO2 particles were found to effectively suppress the coffee-ring, leading to a plate-like deposit morphology with a more uniform color. In general, hydrophilic particles usually prefer to a ring formation, like the SiO2 particles (θWCA = 25) with hydroxyl group in this study. They can be migrated by an outward evaporation-induced flow to the vicinity of contact line and then accumulate at the droplet periphery [51]. For more hydrophilic CDs@SiO2 particles (θWCA = 13.7° in Fig. 4 f), the homogenous deposition can be attributed to the change of the interactions between particles and other components determined by the surface chemistry of particles. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the covalent coupling of CDs onto SiO2 particles brings many carboxyl and amino groups onto the particle surface, which not only improves their hydrophilicity, but also induces additional particle–particle or even particle–substrate attractions due to the existence of massive hydrogen bonding interactions. As a result, the CDs@SiO2 particles would tend to strongly aggregate and adhere to the substrate during the evaporation, significantly suppressing the particle migration to facilitate the formation of a closely packed homogenous structure. Such a mechanism was once reported for the evaporation of charged or attractive colloidal particles [52].

MD simulations of the evaporation of Lennard-Jones nanodroplets have been further employed to provide a deeper understanding of the underlying mechanism. With the increase of the particle–fluid εPW and particle–particle εPP interaction parameters, suspended particles become more hydrophilic and attractive to each other, which qualitatively mimics the decoration of CDs onto SiO2 particles in our experiments. Their deposit morphologies and corresponding characterizations are presented in Fig. 4 g and 4h, respectively. At εPW = 0.6, the hydrophobic interactions drive the particles to absorb and aggregate at the liquid–gas interface, resulting a relatively uniform deposition after the evaporation. While for the hydrophilic particles with εPW = 0.9, they prefer to stay into the droplets to maximizing the favorable contact with solvents, and slightly flow toward the droplet periphery, leaving a ring-like pattern. Further increasing the interaction parameters to εPW = 1.2 and εPP = 1.5, the particle aggregates during the evaporation process, and finally shrinks to a closely packed deposit in the droplet center. The tendency is quantitatively in consistent with our experimental results, giving a microscale insight into how the particle surface property affects the deposition behavior of colloidal droplets.

It is worth indicating that CPCs can effectively enhance the PL intensity of CDs [53]. To this end, we construct 3D CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs with dual optical properties of vivid structural color and bright fluorescence. Aided by the droplet microfluidic technology, CDs@SiO2 latex forms uniform spherical droplets in a continuous and controllable manner, allowing high-throughput production of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs after confined assembly [54]. Monodispersed CDs@SiO2 particles (precursor: CA and urea, precursor concentration: 0.05 wt%) with size of 204, 246, and 277 nm were assembled into blue, green, and red CPC supraballs, respectively. Interestingly, CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs present brighter and more saturated structural color than that of corresponding SiO2 CPC supraballs, which is consistent with the above results of CPC films. SEM images verify that these supraballs exhibit highly uniform spherical morphology with size around 400 µm. In addition, both the cross-section and surface of the CPC supraball show ordered and close-packed regular hexagon arrangement ( Fig. S15 ). These CPC supraballs also show dazzling blue fluorescence under UV excitation. It should be noted that the CPC supraballs with blue structural color exhibit stronger fluorescence than that of others (Fig. 5
a). It is known that the PBG of CPCs is capable of modifying the photon density of states (DOS). The high DOS near the PBG promotes the coupling of emitted photons, efficiently exciting and extracting the fluorescence of CDs [55]. Thus. when the Bragg peak of the CDs@SiO2 CPCs is matched with the PL peak of CDs (445 nm), the highest emission enhancement of CDs would be achieved (Fig. 5 b). Thus, the fluorescence intensity of blue CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs (PBG of 452 nm) is prominently enhanced compared to green and red CPC supraballs (Fig. 5 c). Moreover, the excellent optical stability of these CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs is highly demanded in further applications ( Fig. S16 ).

As identified by all the above results, the one-pot hydrothermal strategy offers an effective way to realize the nano-micro interconnection between SiO2 particles and CDs, thereby generating an ideal building block for CPCs, which exhibit dual optical properties of fluorescence and structural color. If a detection test is carried out using the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs as an individual detecting unit, the accuracy of detection will be greatly improved. MG, a toxic triphenylmethane chemical, is widely used in animal husbandry and aquaculture [56], which has inevitably brought serious threats to human health and ecological environment. Therefore, the concentration detection of MG in water environment is of great significance. Hence, we explored the concentration detection of MG by using the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs. The detection limit of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs was first settled as 0.003 mM ( Fig. S17 ). Strikingly, both structural color and fluorescence gradually changes as the MG concentration increases from 0 to 0.015 mM. Typically, the blue CDs@SiO2 CPC supraball becomes green little by little, the green CDs@SiO2 CPC supraball increasingly turns to yellow, and the bright red structural color of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraball gradually becomes weak blue resulted from second-order diffraction [57]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs was quenched to different degrees under different MG concentrations (Fig. 5 d,
Fig. S18 ). Stem-Volmer equation was attained by data fitting of the change rate of fluorescence intensity of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs under different MG concentrations [58]:

Where I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs before and after injecting MG, CMG is the MG concentration (Fig. 6
a). Thus, the quenching constant (KSV) of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs corresponding to MG is settled as 4.3 × 104 L/mol. The quenching rate constant (Kq) is 0.66 × 1013 L/(mol·s) by calculation (Kq = KSV/τ0, τ0 is the original value of fluorescence lifetime), which is much greater than the upper limit of the typical collision quenching rate constant (1.0 × 1010 L/(mol·s)). As expected, in the MG-induced fluorescence quenching process, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs basically stabilized at 6.5 ns (Fig. 6 b,
Fig. S19 ), indicating that there is a simple intermolecular interaction between MG and the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs [59]. Overall, the fluorescence quenching mechanism can be attributed to the formation of non-luminescent complex (static quenching) [60]. MG with positive charge, which could be adsorbed onto the surface of CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs by electrostatic interactions, caused the static fluorescence quenching based on charge transfer [61]. Meanwhile, a Bragg’s law was proposed to validate the structural color response of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs towards MG [62,63].

Where λ is the PBG of CPCs, D is the size of building blocks, φ is the volume fraction of SiO2 particles and sets as 0.74 (according the FCC structure of the CPC supraball), nc and nm are the RI of SiO2 particles (nc = 1.45) and matrix, respectively. Thus, the structural color response of the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs could be ascribed to the change of effective RI (inter-particle air gaps occupied by MG), as illustrated in Fig. 6 d.

Furthermore, a dual-signal detection chip was developed for high-precision veterinary drug concentration detection. Blue, green and red CPC supraballs are fixed in turn into lily petal shaped chamber of chip template, and served as a detecting unit array, respectively ( Fig. S20 ). Veterinary drug is injected into the chip, and the resulting optical signal is compared and analyzed with the reference signal (colorimetric card) to determine its concentration (Fig. 6 c and 6d). The unit arrays effectively avoid the interference of experimental error and ensure the detection accuracy. For instance, both structural color and fluorescence of each CPC supraballs in the detection chip show obvious changes when MG was injected (Fig. 6 e). By comparing the optical and fluorescence images of CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs with reference signals, we can roughly deduce the concentration of MG. Additionally, we can quantitatively analyze the variation of PBG and PL intensity with the assistance of the reflection and PL spectra ( Fig. S21 ). With the increase of the MG concentration from 0 to 0.015 mM, the position of the reflection peak of blue, green, and red CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs showed 52, 43 and 53 nm red shift, respectively. Besides, the PL intensity was gradually decreased due to the fluorescence quenching effect (Fig. 6 f and 6g). Furthermore, we conducted the selection and interference tests for the bi-optical signal detection chip using MB, CV, and PEF. Surprisingly, the CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs showed structural color and fluorescence differences in response to MG, PEF, CV, and MB, which revealed the selective advantage in veterinary drug detection of the bi-optical signal detection chip. As shown in Fig. S22 , CV and MB would induce slightly weaker structural color response but stronger fluorescence quenching compared with MG. While the PEF caused little structural color and fluorescence response of CDs@SiO2 CPC supraballs. More significantly, the presence of PEF, MB and CV would also arouse different structural color and fluorescence responses on the detection results towards MG. The bi-optical signal detection chip shows the ability to exclude certain interferences in the MG detection according the structural color and fluorescence dual-optical responses ( Fig. S23 ). These promising universality and anti-interference performance of the bi-optical signal detection chip would drive the development of more powerful detection tool and provide meaningful guidance for the development of dual-signal detection chip. The bi-optical signal detection chip converts drug concentration into visual structural color and fluorescence signal, as well as digital reflection peak and PL intensity, which can guarantee the detection in a high-precision fashion.

In summary, a novel in-situ particle engineering strategy was proposed for covalent coupling between SiO2 particle and CDs towards bi-optical CDs@SiO2 CPCs. SiO2 particle and precursor of CDs undergo a bottom-up reaction to form fluorescent CDs@SiO2 particles. Robust covalent interaction enable the nano-micro coupling of CDs and SiO2 particles, and nearly twofold fluorescence enhancement of CDs was obtained derived from the electromagnetic resonance of the dielectric SiO2 nanoparticles. The resultant CDs@SiO2 particles provide hydrogen bond-driven assembly for high-quality CPCs without coffee ring effect, which was demonstrated via MD simulations. It is worth noting that the CDs@SiO2 CPCs show enhanced bi-optical properties based on the optical coupling between CPCs and CDs, namely, more vivid and saturated structural color and brighter fluorescence. In addition, the microfluidics-derived CDs@SiO2 bi-optical CPC supraballs can be applied in veterinary drug concentration detection. By designing a bi-optical signal detection chip, high-precision detection is achieved through visual structural color and fluorescence changes, as well as digital reflection peak and PL intensity variation. This work will stimulate the development of high-precision detecting technique based on bi-optical signal response. Inorganic functional particles have shown strong application potential in chemical, biological and pharmaceutical fields because of their controllable structure, diverse surface chemistry and unique optical and physical properties. We believe this work will also give a methodological guidance and laid the foundation for further industrial application for functional inorganic particle.
